data.title: Indonesia ends elephant rides nationwide
data.description:
(NewsNation) — Tourists looking to ride elephants in Indonesia will no longer be able to do so as the country’s government has officially banned the practice.

The decision to end the popular tourism attraction throughout the entire archipelago is being applauded by animal rights activists who have long condemned the practice. World Animal Protection reports that in December, the Ministry of Forestry’s Directorate General of Natural Resources and Ecosystem Conservation issued Circular Letter No. 6 of 2025, formally ending elephant riding at all conservation and tourism facilities across the country.

According to the nonprofit, facilities that previously offered elephant rides are being directed to shift its tourism toward ethical, observation-based experiences. Those who fail to comply risk having their operating permits revoked. Mason Elephant Park in Bali, one of the final venues in Indonesia to offer elephant rides, ceased the activity Jan. 25 after receiving formal warnings, according to Australian Leisure Management.

“We congratulate the Indonesian Government on taking this world-leading step to safeguarding the dignity of wild animals,” Suzanne Milthorpe, head of campaigns for World Animal Protection, wrote. “The ending of elephant rides in Indonesia sends a strong signal to the wider tourism industry that we’re entering a new chapter of more responsible wildlife tourism.”

The organization and other animal rights groups, such as PETA, have issued numerous reports over the years detailing harmful treatment and training practices behind elephant rides. These tactics have ranged from physical abuse to the withholding of food and veterinary care.

data.title: Trump is sleepwalking into a forever-war in Europe
data.description:
Tragically, we have witnessed this trainwreck coming for months. Former President Joe Biden’s “just enough” strategy of aiding Ukraine in its resistance of a Russian invasion now risks becoming President Trump’s forever-war in Europe.

As Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky left Davos following his meeting with Trump last week, he told reporters that a document with the U.S. dealing concerning security guarantees for Ukraine was “done.” Zelensky emphasized that “For us, security guarantees are first and foremost guarantees from the United States. The document is 100 percent ready, and we are waiting for our partners to confirm the date and place for its signing.”

Trump added that a settlement for the Ukraine war “is coming very soon.”

Special Envoy Steve Witkoff and Trump’s son-in-law Jared Kushner sped off to Moscow to brief Russian President Vladimir Putin, then proceeded to the United Arab Emirates to participate in the trilateral talks.

The talks commenced and concluded in Abu Dhabi the next day. The outcome? Russia refused to back off their maximalist demands and continued to demand Ukraine unilaterally withdraw from the Donbas.

The U.S. is now 0 for 7 in its negotiations with Russia to end the war — chiefly because Ukraine stubbornly refuses to commit national suicide.

Kyiv will not give Moscow in negotiations what the Russians cannot take on the battlefield. Nor should they be persuaded or coerced into doing so. 

The Fortress Belt in the Donetsk Oblast is where Ukraine has drawn the line. It is key terrain, their no-penetration line. It is essential to the defense of Ukraine — protecting key avenues of approach towards Kyiv and Odesa.

In the Donbas, Team Zelensky has adopted the U.S. Army motto: “This we’ll defend!” Embracing the U.S. Army mission to “safeguard the nation, protect its citizenry, and uphold its values.” 

Secretary of the Army Daniel P. Driscoll joined Witkoff and Kushner in the trilateral talks. Do you suppose that resonated with the former Army officer as they applied pressure upon Ukraine to cede the Donbas to Russia?

On Tuesday, Trump pulled a 180. The security guarantees he offered were contingent upon Ukraine withdrawing from the Donbas. That is a hard no for Zelensky and for the Ukrainian people.

On the legal side, Ukrainian law prevents holding a referendum on issues that could threaten the country’s territorial integrity. Then there is public opinion. A poll conducted by the Kyiv International Institute of Sociology in December revealed that 75 percent of Ukrainians “rejected a proposed peace plan that would involve the withdrawal of Ukrainian forces from Donbas.”

The harder Russia pushes back, the harder the Trump administration appears to come back and lean on Ukraine. It is just a matter of time before Trump will again accuse Zelensky of “not wanting peace” and being an “obstructionist.”

Russia wants to make a deal with the U.S., only to secure that deal, Trump needs Zelensky to stop resisting. 

For now, the sticking point is the Donbas. But what if Ukraine agreed to withdraw from the region? Would Russia end the war? Probably not. It will just lead to the next bone of contention.

Three come to mind: As it has in the past, Russia will balk about weapons systems provided to Ukraine for its defense, the physical presence of NATO or European Forces as peacekeepers, and the size of the Ukrainian military.

And while we wait for the ‘next round’ of negotiations to take place, Russia will continue to strike Ukrainian cities and their energy infrastructure with ballistic missiles and drones.

As we warned three years ago, Putin has weaponized winter. Despite this and other war crimes he has committed, he is unlikely to be held accountable despite an arrest warrant issued by the International Criminal Court.

Especially since Team Trump wants to quickly end the war so Washington can secure economic opportunities.

Putin’s military cannot win on the battlefield. It has suffered more than 1.2 million casualties to date. Instead, he is waging a terror war against unarmed civilians in their homes, killing 6,576 and wounding 27,931 since 2023.

We assess it may take three to five years to secure the Donbas at a cost of hundreds of thousands of additional Russian soldiers. They just sustained 415,000 casualties during the first year of Trump’s second term.

Putin is the ‘root cause’ of the war. The killing stops when Putin stops attacking. 

Witkoff’s negotiations have done nothing to bring the war to an end. They only prolong it, creating a “forever-war.”

Trump has the cards to end the war, but those cards need to be played against Russia and not Ukraine. He must coerce Russia to stop attacking, give up their territorial aspirations for the Donbas, and accept a European military peace-keeping force in Ukraine. 

That will likely require military force. It begins with a NATO-enforced no-fly zone over western Ukraine, sufficiently arming Kyiv to defeat Russian forces in Ukraine, and destroying Moscow’s ability to fund and sustain the war. 

Anything less equals a Team Trump forever war in Europe.

Col. (Ret.) Jonathan Sweet served 30 years as a military intelligence officer and led the U.S. European Command Intelligence Engagement Division from 2012 to 2014. Mark Toth writes on national security and foreign policy.

data.title: White House, Senate navigate another shutdown dilemma
data.description:
Morning Report is The Hill’s a.m. newsletter. Subscribe here.

In today’s issue:

▪ White House, Senate leaders eye last-minute deal

▪ FBI carries out search warrant at election office

▪ Fears renewed around political violence

▪ Next “No Kings” protest planned

Democrats and Republicans are wrestling over a potential funding solution that is unlikely to prevent at least part of the government shutting down for several days but could provide a way forward.

The White House and Senate leaders are reportedly eyeing a plan to split off funding for the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), passing a handful of other longer-term bills to fund various agencies while giving both sides more time to negotiate over new limits on federal immigration agents.

Two officials familiar with the proposal told The New York Times that the plan would have the Senate split off the DHS bill from the rest of the package and pass the remaining bills before the Friday night deadline.

Congress would also consider a short-term funding measure to prevent an interruption to other DHS agencies and give time to draft a new funding bill, the Times reported.

The officials emphasized the deal hadn’t yet been reached and hurdles remain. CNN reported that both sides are working to resolve final sticking points ahead of Friday night’s shutdown deadline.

Splitting up the current six-bill package would likely require the House, which is set to return on Monday several days after the funding deadline, to pass the new five-bill package as well as any stopgap for DHS.

It’s also unclear how these negotiations may affect the vote on the entire six-bill package scheduled for today.

Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) on Wednesday laid out Democrats’ demands for changes to immigration enforcement policy that must be made for them to be willing to support a bill to fund DHS.

The demands include ending U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officers’ roving patrols and tightening rules concerning their use of warrants; establishing a universal code of conduct governing federal law enforcement officers’ use of force; and prohibiting federal officers from wearing masks and requiring them to wear body cameras and proper identification.

The Hill’s Alexander Bolton reports a group of Republican senators said they’re open to splitting the DHS bill from the rest of the package.

“Sen. Thune can’t admit it because he’s got to act like a tough guy and the White House is negotiating and all of that, but most of you have been around here for a while and most of us have seen this vampire movie before,” Sen. John Kennedy (R-La.) told reporters.

“I think a rational person would say let’s pass what we can and work on the others,” he added.

A senior GOP senator told Bolton that Senate Republicans would prefer to keep the six-bill package together but would probably agree to leave the DHS measure out if Democrats dig in.

Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) reiterated his preference is to keep together the six-bill package funding the remaining parts of the government through the rest of fiscal 2026 but he didn’t rule out moving forward with the five appropriations bills along with a continuing resolution to cover DHS while negotiations take place on reforms.

Thune told reporters that Senate Democrats must negotiate with the White House if a shutdown is to be avoided. The president hasn’t yet publicly weighed in on the funding battle.

Meanwhile, Democrats are seeking to present a united front as they home in on DHS as the only issue to solve before fully funding the government in the aftermath of the killing of ICU nurse Alex Pretti in Minneapolis.

They’ve generally been successful in doing that so far. After Pretti’s death, Democrats from across the political spectrum came out against the DHS funding bill, saying they could not vote for it in its current form and insisting on new reforms for ICE and U.S. Customs and Border Protection.

Only Sen. John Fetterman (D-Pa.), among the more common contrarians within the caucus, has come out to say he opposes the idea of forcing a government shutdown over the DHS funding debate. He has slammed the ICE operations in Minnesota and joined other Democrats in calling for Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem to resign.

On the ground in Minnesota, White House border czar Tom Homan plans to hold a press conference this morning amid a push to cool tensions in the state. Homan has spoken to Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D), describing those conversations as productive.

▪ The Hill: House GOP warns Senate against changing DHS bill.

▪ The Hill: Senate leaders disagree on who should be negotiating.

Smart Take with Blake Burman

Trump is continuing to warn Iran, saying an “armada,” bigger than the one used in Venezuela, remains on standby in the Middle East. While the commander in chief could soon have a decision to make on potential strikes, State Department deputy principal spokesperson Tommy Pigott told me Wednesday night the context for Iran includes the “maximum pressure” policy over the past year.

“We had a reinstating of maximum pressure under President Trump from day one. That includes sanctions policies that include denying the regime the revenues necessary to fund their malign activities,” Pigott said.

“The president is monitoring this very closely, and he retains his options,” the spokesperson added.

What Trump decides to do next is unclear. What stands out to me right now is how he’s currently using similar language to what he was saying before his final decision on Venezuela.

Burman hosts “The Hill” weeknights, 6p/5c on NewsNation.

3 Things to Know Today

  1. The Trump administration’s deployment of National Guard troops to various cities cost taxpayers nearly half a billion dollars through the end of 2025, according to a new report from the Congressional Budget Office. Officials have sent troops to six cities since June.
  2. The Supreme Court will consider whether to take up Trump’s appeal of the verdict finding him liable for sexually abusing E. Jean Carroll on Feb. 20. Trump has maintained he did not assault Carroll.
  3. Join the politics discussion on the next edition of The Morning Review, a new weekly interactive session with “Whole Hog Politics” author Chris Stirewalt and The Hill’s Bill Sammon, tomorrow morning. Click here to register.

Leading the Day

FBI SEARCH: Tensions over the results of the 2020 presidential election arose again Wednesday as FBI agents executed a search warrant at the elections office in Fulton County, Ga.

An FBI statement revealed that agents carried out a “court authorized law enforcement action” at the county’s main office in Union City, south of Atlanta.

“Our investigation into this matter is ongoing so there are no details that we can provide at the moment,” the statement said.

Fulton County was a centerpiece of Trump’s false claims of voter fraud costing him reelection in 2020, and he has continued to rail against the electoral practices in the county since then.

Democrats slammed the search as threatening voters’ ability to cast their ballot and a government overreach.

“Every Georgian should be alarmed that Donald Trump is wielding federal law enforcement to push his baseless, dangerous lies about winning the 2020 election,” Georgia Democratic Party Chair Charlie Bailey said in a statement.

“This is an assault on your vote,” Fulton County Commissioner Mo Ivory said.

The Department of Justice (DOJ) filed a complaint against Fulton County last month for information related to the 2020 presidential election.

The lawsuit requested the county turn over “all used and void ballots, stubs of all ballots, signature envelopes, and corresponding envelope digital files” from the 2020 general election. The request is to ensure “compliance with federal law,” according to the complaint.

The county also has personal significance for Trump, as Fulton County District Attorney Fani Willis (D) indicted him in 2023 on racketeering charges, alleging he and 18 others conspired to undermine former President Biden’s victory in Georgia in 2020.

The case was dropped in November with Trump back in office.

The search will almost certainly raise anxiety and doubts about the integrity of the electoral process, potentially from both sides of the political aisle.

Polls have shown many of Trump’s supporters still question Biden’s victory in 2020. Meanwhile, Democrats have recently raised concerns about the administration potentially trying to interfere in the midterm elections in November, pointing at the DOJ’s wide-ranging efforts to gain access to voter rolls.

▪ WABE: Appeals court upholds parts of Georgia election law.

NEW VIDEO: Footage emerged Wednesday revealing that Pretti, the man killed by federal immigration agents in Minneapolis over the weekend, brawled with officers 11 days before his death during a protest.

The Associated Press reported the videos show the agents took Pretti to the ground after he kicked out a taillight of their vehicle. A family representative confirmed to CBS News that the man in the videos is Pretti and the family knew about the incident previously.

“A week before Alex was gunned down in the street — despite posing no threat to anyone — he was violently assaulted by a group of (Immigration and Customs Enforcement) agents,” Steve Schleicher, an attorney representing Pretti’s parents, said in a statement. “Nothing that happened a full week before could possibly have justified Alex’s killing at the hands of ICE on Jan 24.”

A DHS spokesperson told NewsNation’s Kellie Meyer that Homeland Security Investigations is investigating the new video.

AGENTS ON LEAVE: The two federal agents who shot Pretti were placed on administrative leave amid an investigation into their conduct.

A DHS official told NewsNation, The Hill’s partner network, that they were placed on leave on Saturday, which is standard policy for officer-involved shootings. They said this is different from restrictive duty, which would result in losing their gun, badge or pay.

DHS spokesperson Tricia McLaughlin separatelytold HuffPost that the ICE officer who killed Renee Good earlier this month in Minneapolis had also been placed on leave, though she did not specify when that had taken place.

The Trump administration has generally taken a different approach to Pretti’s killing, with Trump and other officials saying they support an investigation taking place following bipartisan calls for transparency.

Still, the president and others have sought to shift blame onto Pretti for having a gun with him. Trump said Pretti shouldn’t have had a firearm with him, while FBI Director Kash Patel said people don’t have the right to “incite violence” at protests.

Pretti was licensed to carry his gun, and he didn’t appear to take it out prior to being shot, according to eyewitness videos.

Some gun rights groups have pushed back on officials’ comments about Pretti having a gun.

“Carrying an extra magazine implies nothing,” the National Association for Gun Rights wrote on the social platform X in response to Patel’s remarks. “Holsters designed to carry spare magazines are common and widely sold. Training resources and guides across the internet actively recommend it. Thousands of law-abiding Americans do this every day. This is standard, not overkill.”

▪ The Hill: Judge blocks removal of 5-year-old in Minnesota.

▪ The Hill: Tom Homan says meetings with state officials have been productive.

OBAMA FACTOR: Former President Obama weighed in Wednesday calling attention to ways for those wanting to push back on ICE activities to get involved.

The former president highlighted a story from Vox about how to help the “resistance to ICE” in Minneapolis and beyond.

“In Minneapolis and many other communities across the country, we’re seeing people come together to peacefully protest and support their neighbors. If you’re looking to get involved, here are some ways to help,” he said in a post on X.

FED INTEREST RATE HOLDS: The Federal Reserve voted to keep interest rates steady Wednesday amid pressure from the Trump administration to move quicker in reducing them.

The Federal Open Market Committee voted 10-2 to keep the baseline interest rate range at 3.5 to 3.75 percent. Fed board members Christopher Waller and Stephen Miran voted to cut rates by 0.25 points.

Economists had expected the Fed to keep interest rates at their current point after three straight cuts that started in September. But the decision took on greater prominence as it was the first one since the DOJ was revealed to be investigating Fed Chair Jerome Powell.

The probe is apparently looking into Powell’s congressional testimony about the cost of ongoing renovations to the Fed’s headquarters. But Powell publicly denounced the investigation as one designed to pressure him and the Fed to yield to the president’s wishes on interest rates.

He declined to comment on the probe during his press conference following the Fed’s vote, but he defended the role that the agency plays in keeping the economy stable.

▪ Politico: How Trump is overshadowing the Fed rate decision.

RUBIO TESTIFIES: Secretary of State Marco Rubio asserted the Trump administration reserves the right to attack Venezuela again if its acting president, Delcy Rodríguez, doesn’t cooperate with the U.S.

Rubio told senators during a hearing before the Senate Foreign Relations Committee that military action remains on the table, though the administration isn’t currently positioned to take such action. His statement appears to be a stronger assertion of the possibility of U.S. intervention after Rubio previously told senators that military operations in Venezuela would be limited to the raid to capture former President Nicolás Maduro.

Rubio also set low expectations for the potential pace of change in the South American country. He laid out the administration’s plan for Venezuela to transition from an authoritarian regime to a democracy, but he said he couldn’t give a timeline.

▪ The Hill: Five takeaways from Rubio’s hearing.

▪ The Hill: Rubio, Sen. Rand Paul (R-Ky.) argue if Maduro’s capture was act of war.

IRAN THREATS: Tensions with Iran are on the rise again after Trump warned the Middle Eastern country that the U.S. may launch an attack “far worse” than the strikes conducted last summer.

“Hopefully Iran will quickly ‘Come to the Table’ and negotiate a fair and equitable deal – NO NUCLEAR WEAPONS – one that is good for all parties. Time is running out, it is truly of the essence!” he said in a Truth Social post Wednesday.

Trump has increasingly clashed with Iranian leaders since he seemingly backed away from the possibility of striking Iran over the rising death toll amid the nationwide protests that the regime sought to crush. U.S. assets have been traveling to the region, leading countries in the area to brace for a possible attack.

Iran’s mission to the United Nations warned in response to Trump’s comments that it would respond “like never before” if the U.S. launches a preemptive attack.

▪ The Hill: Rubio raises possibility of striking Iran.

When & Where

  • The president will receive an intelligence briefing at 10 a.m. He will participate in a Cabinet meeting at 11 a.m. and make an announcement at 4 p.m. He and first lady Melania Trump will attend a screening of the documentary film, “Melania,” at 7 p.m. at the Kennedy Center.
  • The Senate will meet at 10:30 a.m.
  • The House is out today.

Zoom In

SAFETY CONCERNS: The recent attacks against two members of Congress have renewed worries about officials’ safety in a hyperpolarized political environment.

Rep. Ilhan Omar (D-Minn.) was sprayed by a man with an unknown liquid substance Tuesday night while speaking at a town hall about the situation in Minneapolis, just days after Rep. Maxwell Frost (D-Fla.) was punched in the face by a man who told him he would be deported.

Both lawmakers were ultimately all right, but the incidents demonstrate the dangers that politicians are facing as political violence becomes more common, The Hill’s Sudiksha Kochi reports.

Many Republicans spoke out to condemn the attack on Omar after it happened, saying violence is unacceptable regardless of political disagreements. Trump, who has been a target of political violence in the past, claimed without evidence that the incident was staged.

The number of reported threats against lawmakers are also on the rise. U.S. Capitol Police reported Tuesday the number of threat assessment cases rose for the third consecutive year in 2025, reaching nearly 15,000 concerning statements, behavior and communications against members of Congress, their families, staff and the Capitol Complex.

▪ The Hill: Alleged Omar attacker has criminal history, supported Trump.

▪ The Hill: FBI taking over Omar investigation.

KENNEDY CENTER RESIGNATION: The head of artistic programming at the Kennedy Center has resigned less than two weeks after his appointment was first announced, multiple outlets reported.

Kevin Couch was named the center’s new senior vice president of artistic programming in a release in mid-January, a development that the center highlighted just one week ago on X. But Couch has now resigned, adding another exit and more turmoil to the center that has undergone significant changes over the past year.

That includes the center’s board voting to change the name to the Trump-Kennedy Center, though any official change must come from Congress.

Couch confirmed to multiple outlets that he left his role but declined to share a reason.

Many high-profile acts have been cutting their ties with the center over the past year, and that has been particularly renewed in recent weeks following the name change push.

Composer Philip Glass became the latest to withdraw on Tuesday when he pulled his newest symphony that was set to premiere at the center in June.

Elsewhere

‘NO KINGS’ PROTESTS CONTINUE: Organizers are planning an additional round of “No Kings” protests in the spring over Good’s and Pretti’s deaths in Minneapolis, building on the multiple such demonstrations opposing the Trump administration throughout 2025.

The next protest is scheduled for March 28 in the Twin Cities in Minnesota, which has become ground zero for the administration’s immigration enforcement crackdown. Leaders said the next protests were already in the process of being planned but shifted to focus on Minneapolis after the killings by federal agents in the city.

Nationwide protests drew millions of demonstrators on a few occasions throughout last year, starting with June 14, which is Trump’s birthday and the day that he held a large military parade in Washington, D.C. Another protest took place in October.

Organizers said they expect the next protest to be the largest yet.

▪ Time: Calls for general strike against ICE.

The Closer

And finally … It’s Thursday, which means it’s time for the weekly news quiz!

DHS and the agencies under it have been the key issue in the pending partial government shutdown, so this quiz will test your knowledge of the department and its history.

Be sure to email your responses to jgans@thehill.com— please add “Quiz” to your subject line. Winners who submit correct answers will receive deserved newsletter fame on Friday.

After which event was DHS formed?

1. The Oklahoma City bombing

2. The 1998 World Trade Center bombing

3. The 9/11 attacks

4. Hurricane Katrina

Which agency does NOT fall under the jurisdiction of DHS?

1. The U.S. Secret Service

2. The U.S. Coast Guard

3. The National Security Agency

4. The Federal Emergency Management Agency

Which of these Cabinet departments has a larger workforce than DHS?

1. Veterans Affairs

2. State

3. Justice

4. Health and Human Services

How many permanent, confirmed Homeland Security secretaries have there been?

1. 8

2. 12

3. 16

4. 18

data.title: Chia seeds sold by Amazon, Whole Foods recalled over salmonella risk
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – A “superfood” sold nationwide is under recall over the risk of salmonella contamination.

California-based Navitas Organics is recalling certain eight-ounce packages of organic chia seeds after its supplier notified the company of a recall.

The seeds, which are advertised on the product pouches as a “plant-based superfood,” were sold online by retailers including Amazon, and at Whole Foods and other retailers across the country.

The recall includes stand up pouches with the UPC 858847000284 with the following lot codes listed above the nutritional information:

  • W31025283 – Best If Used By: End APR 2027
  • W31025286 – Best If Used By: End APR 2027
  • W31025287 – Best If Used By: End APR 2027
  • W31025311 – Best If Used By: End MAY 2027
  • W31025314 – Best If Used By: End MAY 2027
  • W31025315 – Best If Used By: End MAY 2027
  • W31025316 – Best If Used By: End MAY 2027
  • W31025317 – Best If Used By: End MAY 2027

The recall was done out of precaution, and the Food and Drug Administration has not reported any illnesses or medical events tied to the product as of Wednesday.

“Navitas has provided Organic Chia Seeds to our customers for more than 20 years. Providing safe, healthy food for our customers is why we have been in business this long and it’s why we are recalling this product today” CEO Ira Haber said in a statement.

Salmonella bacteria are a leading cause of infection in the U.S. and can cause serious illness or death among young children, adults over the age of 65 and people with a weakened immune system, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

The most common ways of contracting salmonella include eating contaminated food; drinking or having contact with contaminated water; and touching animals, animal poop and the environment where they live.

Symptoms usually include diarrhea, fever and stomach cramps.

Customers who purchased the seeds can return them to the place of purchase for a refund. Anyone with questions or a request for replacement is encouraged to call 1 (855) 215-5702.

data.title: Congress lurches toward government shutdown as leaders swap demands
data.description:
Congress lurched a day closer to a partial government shutdown on Wednesday after Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) laid out a list of major reforms that Democrats want to make to the Department of Homeland Security, proposals that will likely encounter resistance from President Trump.

Democrats say they will block the six-bill government funding package the Senate will vote on Thursday, which includes the Homeland Security appropriations bill, putting Washington on the brink of another shutdown less than three months after the longest shutdown in American history.

Schumer is demanding that Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) split off the Homeland Security funding bill from the package and has promised that Democrats will cooperate to pass the other five spending measures by the Jan. 30 deadline.

The Democratic leader says Democrats want to “overhaul” Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) to “ensure the public’s safety.”

Democrats want to end roving ICE patrols, tighten the rules for requiring search warrants before entering migrants’ homes, and “enforce accountability” by establishing a universal code of conduct governing federal officers’ use of force.

They also want to prohibit ICE officers from wearing masks and require them to wear body cameras and proper identification.

“Under President Trump, Secretary Noem and Stephen Miller, ICE has been unleashed without guardrails. They violate constitutional rights all the time and deliberately refuse to coordinate with state and local law enforcement,” Schumer said after meeting with Senate Democrats Wednesday.

“After talking with my caucus, Senate Democrats are united on a set of common-sense and necessary policy goals that we need to rein in ICE and the violence,” Schumer announced.

Republicans need the votes of at least eight Senate Democrats to advance the six-bill funding package that Thune has put on the Senate floor this week. Sen. Rand Paul (R-Ky.), a fiscal conservative, regularly votes against procedural motions to move spending bills.

So far, only one Democrat, Sen. John Fetterman (D-Pa.), has said he will vote to advance the spending package in order to avoid a potential shutdown, even as he objected to ICE’s actions in Minneapolis.

Several Democrats who broke with Schumer in November to vote for a funding deal to reopen the government, say they will vote against the pending package if it includes the Homeland Security funding bill.

“I hate shutdowns, I’m one of the people who helped negotiate the solution to the end of the last shutdown but I can’t vote for a bill that includes ICE funding under these circumstances,” Sen. Angus King (Maine), an independent who caucuses with Democrats and who repeatedly voted to reopen the government, told CBS’s “Face the Nation.”

A group of Senate Republicans, particularly members of the Senate Appropriations Committee, are open to setting aside the Homeland Security funding bill in hopes of getting the other five appropriations bills passed before the weekend.

Sen. John Kennedy (R-La.), a member of the Senate Appropriations Committee, said “a bunch” of Republican senators would be willing to split off the Homeland Security appropriations bill in hopes passing full-year funding for the Departments of Defense, Health and Human Services, Labor, Education, Transportation, State, and financial services-related agencies.

“Sen. Thune can’t admit it because he’s got to act like a tough guy and the White House is negotiating and all of that but most of you have been around here for a while and most of us have seen this vampire movie before,” Kennedy told reporters.

“I think the Democrats don’t want to vote for a Homeland Security bill,” he added. “If that’s the case, then what are our choices? To throw out all the bills? Or one?”

“I think a rational person would say let’s pass what we can and work on the others,” Kennedy said.

A senior Republican senator who requested anonymity to comment on internal discussions said that the Republican leadership would probably agree to leave the Homeland Security funding measure behind to avoid looming shutdown.

“If that was all that was left, we’d probably do it,” said the GOP lawmaker, who noted that Thune hasn’t ruled out moving five of the appropriations bills without Homeland Security funding.

Thune made it clear, however, that he would strongly prefer to move all six appropriations bills passed by the House together this week while the House is on recess.

“We need to fund the government. We have a bill sitting in the Senate today that does that, that if we pick it up and pass it and put it on the president’s desk and all 12 appropriations bills will have been done this year,” he told reporters Wednesday.

He warned that splitting up the six-bill package would delay it and risk the government lapsing into a partial shutdown Saturday.

He argued that amending the legislation on the floor would open the door to other amendments and floor fights — and would require sending it back to the House for approval.

“If the bill goes back to the House of Representatives … if the bill changes much from what the House already passed, then you’ve got another steep hill to climb over there,” Thune said at a Wednesday press conference.  

“All of this just drags things out and delays us funding the government. The longer things are delayed around here, we end up in another government shutdown,” he said.

House Republicans warned on Wednesday that any changes the Senate makes to the six-bill funding package, such as stripping out the Homeland Security appropriations title, would not clear the House and would lead to a government shutdown.

House conservatives said they would seek significant concessions from Democrats in exchange for splitting up the six-bill minibus, which Democratic appropriators had negotiated and signed off on before Alex Pretti was shot and killed by a Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis this weekend.

“Any changes to the bipartisan bicameral agreement that was made with the Senate after the House has passed it and sent that to the Senate would not be possible,” a House GOP leadership aide told The Hill.

Emily Brooks contributed.

data.title: Trump, tech giants seek to change data center narrative amid growing scrutiny
data.description:
President Trump and major tech companies are attempting to change the narrative around data centers, as rising electricity costs linked to AI infrastructure faces growing local opposition and increasingly becomes a political liability. 

Both the White House and tech industry appear keen to limit the fallout from data centers, with initiatives to bring down electricity prices and recast the sprawling facilities as community players willing to pay their fair share. 

While the Trump administration has been broadly supportive of data centers, including through efforts to fast-track their construction, going forward that support may have limitations. 

“We are going to embrace data centers, but not at the price … of raising costs for consumers,” a senior White House official told The Hill on Tuesday. 

“So if you want to invest and build data centers, you’ve got to do it in the right way, the way that is not going to pass that cost onto consumers,” the official said.  

The aide also said hyperscalers — large cloud services providers like Amazon, Google and Microsoft — should have to bring their own power sources. 

“That’s certainly what the president feels as well — that they should have their own generation,” the official said. 

The White House official said the administration was working with “each of the major hyperscalers and data center operators” to try to find ways to defray costs for consumers. The person declined to name specific companies. 

The comments come as data centers face increasing backlash, including because of their potential impacts on high electricity prices, becoming a potential liability for the GOP.  

The pushback is coming from enemies and allies alike, as even a handful of prominent Republicans raise concerns. 

“Rushed AI expansion and data centers being built all over the country from state to state with no plan in regards to environmental and critical water supply impact has massive future implications and problems,” said then-Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene (R-Ga.), who left Congress amid rifts from her fellow Republicans on issues including data centers and the Epstein files, in an X post last year.

In a rare show of bipartisanship, the Trump administration announced earlier this month that it was joining forces with Pennsylvania Gov. Josh Shapiro (D), Maryland Gov. Wes Moore (D) and then-Virginia Gov. Glenn Youngkin (R) to pressure the nation’s largest regional grid operator to address rising electricity prices. 

They urged grid operator PJM, which serves large swaths of the East Coast and Midwest, to make data centers pay more for new power sources added to the grid than households, unless the data centers provide their own power sources. 

PJM has proposed a more incentive-based approach, offering expedited grid connections for data centers that bring their own power sources. 

The Trump-governor plan also calls on PJM to hold an emergency power auction while PJM’s said it would pursue an “Immediate Initiation” to procure more power. 

If implemented, the Trump-backed plan could help bring down costs, as could PJM’s actual proposal, said Ben Hertz-Shargel, who leads research about the electric grid at Wood Mackenzie. 

“These plans of having an auction and procuring longer term commitments… would be helpful. It would help improve the likelihood of bringing on generation,” he said, but cautioned “it is not the case that this mechanism can allow data centers to get built with no impact on customers.” 

‘Pay their own way’

While the Trump administration has sought to rapidly expand the growth of data centers, in recent weeks, it has shifted its message to emphasize concerns about the impacts these technologies are having on consumers’ pocketbooks. 

“We are the ‘HOTTEST’ Country in the World, and Number One in AI. Data Centers are key to that boom, and keeping Americans FREE and SECURE but, the big Technology Companies who build them must ‘pay their own way,’” Trump argued in a post on Truth Social a few days before the PJM announcement. 

Several tech companies have echoed the president’s language in unveiling their own efforts. Microsoft announced a five-pronged “community first” AI infrastructure, vowing to “pay our own way” to prevent its data centers from raising consumer electricity bills. 

OpenAI made similar commitments for its Stargate project, describing it as a “partnership with communities” and suggesting that “we can only achieve our mission by being good neighbors.” 

Microsoft’s plan includes a commitment to pay utility rates that are “high enough to cover our electricity costs” but doesn’t specify how that would be determined.  OpenAI said its specific commitments would vary region-to-region but could include things like paying for new power sources and grid upgrades, reducing consumption during periods of high demand or “planning transparently and proactively.” 

“This is directly a response to growing backlash,” said Miquel Vila, lead analyst at Data Center Watch, a project from the AI company 10a Labs tracking local opposition to data centers. 

The tide has rapidly turned against data centers over the past year, as local communities have voiced concerns about rising electricity prices, as well as water usage and pollution. 

Data Center Watch found that 20 data center projects worth about $98 billion were blocked or delayed in the face of local opposition between late March and June 2025. 

Asked about community concerns, Cy McNeill, senior director of federal affairs at the Data Center Coalition, which represents the data center industry, emphasized in a statement that these projects can also have benefits like “high-wage jobs” and “significant tax revenue to support schools, transportation, public safety, and other local priorities.”

Experts say currently high power prices are not necessarily because of data centers – but these power-hungry projects could dramatically raise future prices. 

 “Largely, the cost increases that have happened so far are not caused by data centers. They’re caused by investments to replace aging equipment,” Hertz-Shargel said. 

But, he added that the country is at a transition point where in some cases, prices are being driven by “anticipating investments utilities are making for larger loads” like data centers. 

He also noted that the Trump administration “does not really have much jurisdiction” over how power markets handle data centers. But he noted that the independent Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC), which has presidential appointees, could help set common rules for how energy markets deal with large sources of demand like data centers. 

The Trump administration only has so many tools at its disposal to address the data center issue. Frank Felder, principal at Independent Electricity Consultants, said those tools include the “bully pulpit,” which they do appear to be using. 

But, he noted, “they haven’t been willing to tell…the people who run…power markets not to allow data centers to connect until there is new supply.” 

“They could do that, but their economic policy is built around expanding AI and therefore data centers, so that’s not going to happen,” Felder said. 

AI issues loom over midterm elections

The Trump administration has frequently sought to bolster the development of data centers around the country – saying they want to “win the AI race” against China. It has moved to speed up approvals for data centers, including by creating a “categorical exclusion” curtailing environmental reviews of these projects. 

It has also sought to expedite approvals of new chemicals expected to be used for data centers and pushed to try to get data centers connected to the power grid more quickly. 

Data centers are expected to be just one piece of the policy puzzle on rising power prices – as the administration is also setting up barriers for renewable energy development and looking to expand exports of natural gas. 

“The headwinds against renewables … is a big challenge for affordability. Renewables are the technology that is far less expensive to build than natural gas power plants, and they are available now,” said Hertz-Shargel, adding that natural gas also has to compete with “exports, which are growing up rapidly.” 

Many Democratic lawmakers have embraced the resistance to data centers. Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.) has called for a moratorium on data center construction, arguing a halt is necessary to “give democracy a chance to catch up.”  

Sen. Chris Van Hollen (D-Md.) also introduced legislation earlier this month that seeks to limit the impact of data centers on consumer electricity costs by directing states to evaluate the need for new rate classes for data centers and directing to FERC to issue a rule requiring data centers to pay for transmission upgrades. 

“The principle behind this legislation is simple: the huge corporations building and running data centers should cover the costs of the energy they need – not push those costs onto the backs of consumers,” Van Hollen said in a statement at the time.  

Following successes in the 2025 elections, many Democrats see rising electricity prices as a key part of their affordability message heading into the midterms. 

Some of that message has been focused around the administration’s anti-renewable moves. 

At the same time, many Democrats are also generally supportive of data centers, even amid price concerns. For example, over the summer, Shapiro touted a major Amazon investment in his state. 

Republicans — many of whom, like Trump, have frequently embraced the data center buildout — face a complicated endeavor in messaging around these concerns. 

“With populist issues like this, it’s going to be tough to fight back against, because people are seeing their rates go up and … explaining really wonky stuff like electricity rates … is really probably a fool’s errand, because if you’re explaining, you’re losing,” said Republican strategist Christopher Johnson. 

“If you’ve got opposition to local data centers, and it’s becoming a campaign issue in a congressional race, you’re probably not going to be able to get people completely to flip on the issue and go, ‘I love OpenAI,’” he added. “Now what you can do, probably, if you’re the tech folks and if you’re a Republican running for Congress, is turn down the negativity.”

data.title: Trump admin steps back into Native American mascot debate at schools
data.description:
The Trump administration is stepping back into the Native American mascot debate, citing a school with a Title VI violation for changing its logo in accordance with state rules.  

Research has shown that Indigenous mascots have negative effects on Native students, and in the past several years, multiple states have banned them, while some schools have changed their teams on their own in the face of accusations of racism. 

But the ones who switched may need to be prepared to fight Team Trump in the courts, with a Title VI violation resulting in a potential loss of funding if an agreement isn’t reached with the federal government.   

The Education Department cited Connetquot Central School District, on Long Island, N.Y., with a Title VI violation for changing from the “Thunderbirds” to the “T-Birds.” The administration said it was a violation because the school only changed it “solely because it originates from Native American symbolism.”  

The district, however, was only following state rules: New York ordered such changes in 2022.  

John Kane, who is Mohawk and an activist who helped create the new rules in New York, said the whole situation is “absurd,” including Connetquot’s attempt at reform. 

“The whole thing is absurd, because part of what McMahon and Trump are suggesting is that somehow they are discriminating against Native people by removing the mascots — when we’re the ones who called for it,” Kane said, referring to Education Secretary Linda McMahon.

“One thing, I was staunchly opposed to the idea that [New York] Attorney General Letitia James cut a deal with [the school district] to allow them to keep T-Birds in lieu of Thunderbirds. I mean, it’s the same damn thing,” he added. “What Connetquot has used for a mascot is basically a bird in a sweater with a lightning bolt blazing across the front of it, which is, I mean, in its purest sense, it’s a mockery. I mean, the idea of making a cartoon image of a bird in a sweater is by no means any in any way, shape or form, promoting our culture.” 

This isn’t the first time the Trump administration got involved in the issue, supporting the Massapequa School District last year after it fought back against New York’s change.  

The Education Department in June referred the New York Department of Education and the New York State Board of Regents to the Justice Department for a Title VI violation over its refusal to end the rule blocking the mascots.  

A source familiar with the situation said the Justice Department is still investigating the issue.  

“Forcing them to change the name, after all of these years, is ridiculous and, in actuality, an affront to our great Indian population. The School Board, and virtually everyone in the area, are demanding the name be kept,” Trump said in a social media post at the time. 

The National Congress of American Indians found at least 16 schools changed their mascots with Native imagery from March 2022 to April 2023.  

A research analysis published in 2020 that consolidated findings from numerous papers on how Native mascots impact Native and non-Native individuals indicated they led to lower self-esteem and a lower sense of community worth in Native students. One finding also showed individuals associated Native mascots with negative stereotypes, such as being lazy and poor, more often than they do white mascots.

Though calls for changes have accelerated in recent years, they are decades old.

In 2001, the U.S. Commission on Civil Rights released a statement calling for the end of Native mascots at non-native schools. Years later, the commission celebrated the Washington Football Team changing its name in 2020, a shift Trump has also criticized.

“Progress on this issue is being threatened. Throughout our nation’s history, schools have abandoned other racist mascots, like mascots that characterize Black Americans, but progress has just been slower on getting rid of racist mascots that dehumanize Native people and so, yeah, it’s really concerning that as progress is being made, that progress is being attacked,” said Malia Gesuale, staff attorney for the Native American Rights Fund. 

Those who defend the old logos and mascots say they offer a teaching opportunity for students about Native culture, with some defending institutions that have used the same mascot for decades.

One group, the Native American Guardians Association (NAGA), is involved in a lawsuit to stop New York from forcing Massapequa School District to change its mascot. The organization is hoping the lawsuit will go to the Supreme Court, where a win would allow them to go after states who have blocked the use of Native American mascots.  

“When we look at other cultures, we can talk about even the Fighting Irish or the Patriots or, oh gosh, there’s so many that represent schools that aren’t Native American and we would never ask them to remove their representation. So NAGA’s stance is that you’re not going to discriminate against one group, unless you are going to discriminate against all groups, and then what are they going to do? Then there will be no names for schools to have to represent them,” said Becky Clayton-Anderson, president of the group and an enrolled Hidatsa Native American, according to the organization’s website. 

“We are the minority of all minorities, and so, the more they try to erase our imagery at these schools, the more they’re trying to just erase us from history,” she added.

data.title: Jeffries slams Stephen Miller as 'hateful bigot' and 'architect' of DHS 'brutality'
data.description:
House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) labeled White House deputy chief of staff Stephen Miller a “hateful bigot” for his role in shaping immigration policy and called into question Miller’s future in the Trump administration.

Amid mounting scrutiny of Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem’s handling of the fatal officer-involved shootings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti, Jeffries pivoted his attention onto Miller — who also serves as the president’s homeland security adviser and is credited with crafting some of the administration’s most hardline immigration policies.

“Stephen Miller is one of the malignant architects of the violence and brutality DHS has unleashed on the American people,” Jeffries wrote in a post on the social platform X, referring to the Department of Homeland Security.

“He slandered hero nurse Alex Pretti as a would-be assassin,” the Democratic leader continued. “Why is this hateful bigot still Donald Trump’s chief immigration adviser?”

The fatal shooting this past weekend of the 37-year-old Minneapolis resident, an intensive care nurse who worked in a Veterans Affairs hospital, has provoked sharp backlash across the political spectrum and mounting calls for Noem to step down, even from some Republican politicians.

Lawmakers have taken issue particularly with Noem’s remarks immediately after the shooting, when she compared Pretti to a “domestic terrorist” and accused him of “brandishing” a gun, without any evidence.

Miller made similar comments in the immediate aftermath of the shooting, calling Pretti a “would-be assassin” who “tried to murder federal agents.”

Pretti had a valid concealed carry permit, and there is no evidence that Pretti brandished a gun, which multiple videos show an agent removing from his waistband seconds before he was shot. 

The White House dismissed Jeffries’s remarks in a statement to The Hill.

“Hakeem Jeffries is a buffoon who has repeatedly lied about and smeared federal law enforcement officers, including inciting violence against them by encouraging his supporters to ‘fight’ President Trump’s agenda ‘in the streets,’” White House spokesperson Abigail Jackson said.

“No one should take anything he says seriously,” she continued.

data.title: Omar slams Trump's 'reprehensible rhetoric' on immigration in wake of personal attack
data.description:
Rep. Ilhan Omar (D-Minn.) suggested on Wednesday that President Trump’s “reprehensible rhetoric” on immigration has contributed to an environment in which “right-wing grifters” feel free to “terrorize our community.”

Omar spoke at a press conference one day after she was ambushed during a town hall event by a man who shouted at her and sprayed an unknown substance on her. He was tackled to the ground and arrested, and Omar remained unharmed and continued speaking.

Omar nodded to the incident in her brief remarks on Wednesday, as she railed against the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown in Minnesota and the targeting of the Somali community, which has been under intense scrutiny in the wake of the sprawling welfare fraud scandal involving dozens of Somali Americans.

“Karmell Mall is home to more than 100 small businesses, and the owners here have seen a significant decline in business since Trump’s ‘Operation Metro Surge’ began. Community members are afraid to go out as a result of the occupation in our city by ICE,” Omar said at the press conference, which was held at the predominantly Somali shopping center in her district.

“Not only is the federal occupation hurting businesses, the president’s reprehensible rhetoric has let right-wing grifters to show up here to terrorize our community,” Omar continued, a veiled nod to the incident the previous day. “It is indefensible.”

Minneapolis police identified the suspect arrested as 55-year-old Anthony Kazmierczak, 55, who was immediately arrested and booked on a third-degree assault charge.

Kazmierczak has a lengthy criminal record, according to Minnesota court records, and a history of support for Trump and conservative causes, according to a review of his social media.

The Associated Press reported that Kazmierczak has referred to Democrats as “angry and liars” in social media posts and made critical comments about former President Biden.

“Trump wants the US is stronger and more prosperous,” Kazmierczak reportedly wrote in one post. “Stop other countries from stealing from us. Bring back the fear that enemies back away from and gain respect that If anyone threatens ourselves or friends we will (expletive) them up.”

data.title: DC crews pile snow at RFK Stadium site as city slowly digs out of storm
data.description:
Washington, D.C. Mayor Muriel Bowser said on Wednesday that the city has resorted to piling snow at Robert F. Kennedy Stadium amid growing frustration over the slow response to the storm.

“Ice and snow are not melting—so crews are hauling them to the RFK site,” Bowser wrote in a post on the social platform X, including a photo of a mound of snow.

“As of this morning, the mound was two stories high and the length of two football fields,” she continued.

Residents have struggled to walk on sidewalks, drive on roads or take scooters to their metro stops as the frigid temperatures have paralyzed normal operations.

Local public schools are reopening on Thursday but will operate on a two-hour delay for the rest of the week. By Wednesday evening, all major roadways were “passable,” all Metro stations were “clear and ready for operation,” and 53 of the 54 WMATA bus routes were up and running, Bowser said.

Bowser thanked residents for their patience Tuesday night, as officials continued to dig out side and residential roads, saying, “This winter storm was complex, and our response tactics have had to evolve. Ice, sleet, and snow—combined with persistent, below-freezing temperatures—have made clearing efforts particularly difficult.”

“DC, we appreciate your patience—our teams will be out until the work is done,” she continued.

In total, Reagan Washington National Airport, located outside of D.C. in Arlington, Va., recorded more than 6 inches of snow, according to local outlet Fox 5.

Dulles International Airport in Chantilly, Va., saw 7 inches, while Baltimore/Washington International Thurgood Marshall Airport faced more than 11 inches of snowfall, the news outlet said.
